EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level trough stretched from Southern Canada to the Gulf Coast and an abundant amount of moisture was present across the Mid-Atlantic. A surface low was moving across the region in the overnight hours and showers and thunderstorms formed along the attendant cold front and surface trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was estimated to have touched down at approximately 3:31 AM EDT. Peak winds were estimated to have been 65 mph. A large tree was uprooted and fell across Sally Mill Road near the intersection of Light Horse Court. Additional trees and large branches were found uprooted and collapsed in different directions along with branches snapped or twisted at various points on a large residential property along Light Horse Court. The tornado quickly lifted around 3:35 AM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first 90-degree day of August 2014 and one of the few 90-degree days overall of a very cool summer also brought a heat-related child death.  A 2-year old girl left in a parked, closed van was found dead the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke Times reported that a 2-year old girl was found dead on September 1st after being left in a parked, closed van in southeast Roanoke.  The high temperatures on the 31st reached 90 degrees for the first time during the month of August 2014.  The girl was pronounced dead at the scene and apparently had died the day before when the outside air temperature reached 90 degrees in the 3 to 4 pm time frame that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached the region, surface winds increased significantly in advance of the front on Tuesday the 14th, and then continued very gusty behind the front into the early morning hours of Wednesday the 15th. A large number of trees were blown down in association with these winds. Showers and storms developed in advance of the front and also occurred coincident to the passage of the front. Trees were blown down in association with a few of the storms increasing to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree limbs were blown down on North 15th Street. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass slipped into the Southern Appalachian region followed by a strong upper level system that pushed east from the Lower Mississippi Valley.  Sufficient moisture was available for lifting over the dense and cold surface air resulting in an ice storm that produced accretions ranging from around one tenth to as much as one half inch. The area affected extended from the Cumberland Plateau counties closer to the Kentucky border east northeast across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accretion in Jonesville reached one tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough, developing through the central U.S. and swinging into the southeast states the Tuesday before Thanksgiving induced an area of low pressure along the eastern Gulf Coastal region.  As the upper trough shifted into the eastern U.S., the surface low underwent explosive development as it moved northward along the southeast and Mid-Atlantic coastal region.  The deepening surface low pulled cold air and moisture into the region bringing a period of snow to areas west of the Blue Ridge on one of the busiest travel days of the year.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the one to three inch range, but several locations saw snowfall in the three to four inch range across southwest Virginia, northwest North Carolina, and southeast West Virginia.  The heaviest snowfall within southwest Virginia was at Mount Rogers in Grayson county where eight inches of snow was observed.  The heavy wet snow resulted and combined gusty winds resulted in power outages for several thousands residents of southwest Virginia counties.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of mid-morning on the day before Thanksgiving, namely November 26, 2014.  The amounts below represent the maximum amounts reported from these counties.||Alleghany (4E Covington) - 3 inches,|Bath (Warm Springs) - 4 inches,|Bedford (peaks of Otter) - 4 inches,|Bland (5N Bland) - 3 inches,|Botetourt (Fincastle) - 1.5 inch,|Carroll (Hebron) - 4.9 inches,|Craig (2W Simmonsville) - 6.5 inches,|Floyd (Check) - 4.5 inches,|Giles (Mountain Lake) - 5 inches,|Grayson (Mount Rogers) - 8 inches,|Montgomery (2SSW Christiansburg) - 5.8 inches,|Patrick (Meadows of Dan) - 3.8 inches,|Pulaski (2S Snowville) - 6.1 inches,|Roanoke (3ESE Cave Spring) - 2.5 inches,|Rockbridge (Raphine) - 4.0 inches,|Smyth (Sugar Grove) - 3.5 inches,|Tazewell (Burkes Garden) - 1.6 inch,|Wythe (Rural Retreat) - 3.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed 6.1 inches of snow just south of Snowville, while elsewhere around the county snowfall amounts were mostly in the three to four inch range, including the city of Snowville, Draper, and Pulaski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region resulted in light and variable winds. Moisture advection from a coastal low pressure combining with the light winds resulted in an extended period of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surrounding counties reported one-quarter visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region resulted in light and variable winds. Moisture advection from a coastal low pressure combining with the light winds resulted in an extended period of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surrounding counties reported one-quarter visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was lifting northward through the southeast and southern Mid-Atlantic states in advance of a strong cold front and intensifying upper trough.  This pattern allowed unseasonably warm air from the Gulf of Mexico to be brought northward into the region in advance of the front.  Widespread heavy rain showers and even a few thunderstorms spread into the region during the afternoon and evening.  However, one area of thunderstorms across the southern Shenandoah Valley became severe during the late evening hours producing wind damage in eastern Rockbridge county, especially near Buena Vista, where several trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockbridge County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down onto Irish Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow upper trough and associated strong cold front were approaching the region from the west.  Strong southeast winds developed through the mountains of Smyth and Tazewell counties in Virginia into Mercer county in southeast West Virginia during the early morning hours.  The winds were strong enough to blow over a communications tower near Tazewell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tazewell County Emergency Management Director reported that winds had blown over a communications tower near Tazewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very high amplitude upper atmospheric pattern featured equally deep troughs in the western and eastern U.S.  Within the eastern U.S. trough, was a vigorous Alberta clipper that intensified immensely as it plunged into the southeast states into the base of the upper trough.  Meanwhile...a large nearly 1040mb Canadian High was plunging into the central and eastern U.S. on the back side of the developing storm system in the southeast states.  The combination of these features brought an early season snowfall principally to the Appalachian mountains of eastern Tennessee, far southwest Virginia, and northwest North Carolina.  Light snow also fell in the mountains of eastern West Virginia, but the storm system tracked too far south for any significant amounts in that region.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range across this region...with some higher totals in the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina.  Strong and gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow in these areas causing scattered power outages.  A few locations recorded wind gusts in excess of 45 mph, meeting strong wind criteria.  These included Hot Springs in Bath county with a wind gust of 46 mph at 8:35 am EST on 11/2/14, the AWOS at the Galax/Hillsville Airport on 11/2/14 at 12:35 am EST, and a weather station located four miles west of Dublin in Pulaski county which recorded a wind gust of 47 mph on 11/2/14 at 4:05 am EST.  Several hundred residents of Tazewell county in far southwest Virginia were left without power for several hours from the comination of 30 to 40 mph wind gusts and the heavy, wet snow.  Because of warm temperatures within the preceding days and the early season time of the event, roads and travel conditions were not significantly impacted.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of the afternoon of November 1, 2014.  These denote the high end of the event in these counties.  Note, the event actually began during the afternoon of October 31st.||Bland (Ceres) - 1 inch,|Carroll (6N Galax, Woodlawn) - 3 inches,|City of Galax - 4 inches,|Floyd (Floyd) - 3 inches,|Giles (Mountain Lake) - 3 inches,|Grayson (5NE Independence) - 4 inches,|Patrick (Meadows of Dan) - 4 inches,|Pulaski (2S Snowville) - 2 inches,|Smyth (Marion) - 2 inches,|Tazewell (Tazewell) - 4 inches,|Wythe (Rural Retreat) - 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed three inches of snow at Mountain Lake.  The elevation at Mountain Lake is over 4000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation as high pressure retreated to the north of the area and low pressure formed over the Mid-Atlantic region. The northeast flow kept temperatures right around freezing during the evening and overnight hours while the forming low brought plenty of moisture, resulting in on-going precipitation for most of the night and into the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of ice accumulations between a hundredth and two-tenths of an inch were reported around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very high amplitude upper atmospheric pattern featured equally deep troughs in the western and eastern U.S.  Within the eastern U.S. trough, was a vigorous Alberta clipper that intensified immensely as it plunged into the southeast states into the base of the upper trough.  Meanwhile...a large nearly 1040mb Canadian High was plunging into the central and eastern U.S. on the back side of the developing storm system in the southeast states.  The combination of these features brought an early season snowfall principally to the Appalachian mountains of eastern Tennessee, far southwest Virginia, and northwest North Carolina.  Light snow also fell in the mountains of eastern West Virginia, but the storm system tracked too far south for any significant amounts in that region.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range across this region...with some higher totals in the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina.  Strong and gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow in these areas causing scattered power outages.  A few locations recorded wind gusts in excess of 45 mph, meeting strong wind criteria.  These included Hot Springs in Bath county with a wind gust of 46 mph at 8:35 am EST on 11/2/14, the AWOS at the Galax/Hillsville Airport on 11/2/14 at 12:35 am EST, and a weather station located four miles west of Dublin in Pulaski county which recorded a wind gust of 47 mph on 11/2/14 at 4:05 am EST.  Several hundred residents of Tazewell county in far southwest Virginia were left without power for several hours from the comination of 30 to 40 mph wind gusts and the heavy, wet snow.  Because of warm temperatures within the preceding days and the early season time of the event, roads and travel conditions were not significantly impacted.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of the afternoon of November 1, 2014.  These denote the high end of the event in these counties.  Note, the event actually began during the afternoon of October 31st.||Bland (Ceres) - 1 inch,|Carroll (6N Galax, Woodlawn) - 3 inches,|City of Galax - 4 inches,|Floyd (Floyd) - 3 inches,|Giles (Mountain Lake) - 3 inches,|Grayson (5NE Independence) - 4 inches,|Patrick (Meadows of Dan) - 4 inches,|Pulaski (2S Snowville) - 2 inches,|Smyth (Marion) - 2 inches,|Tazewell (Tazewell) - 4 inches,|Wythe (Rural Retreat) - 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported just under two inches of snow just south of Snowville and the public observed just under two inches of snow five miles north of Indian Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation evolved Tuesday as a cold front stalled just to the south of the Mid-Atlantic region and high pressure built in over New England. This synoptic set up resulted in persisting northeasterly flow that kept surface temperatures at or just below freezing while southwesterly flow in the lower levels provided a deep warm layer. This combination of temperatures with the overrunning precipitation resulted in ice accumulations across most of northern and western Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shenandoah National Park reported a thin glaze of ice along roadways, resulting in the closure of Skyline Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving through the Mid-Atlantic brought widespread precipitation that overrode persisting surface cold air, resulting in freezing rain, mainly at elevated locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of between a trace and a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation were reported in surrounding counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys during the day on the 24th, moving into southwest Virginia during the early to mid-afternoon.  Very strong southerly gradient winds were observed in advance of the front, a common occurrence with these type of synoptic patterns across the mountains of southwest Virginia.  Several wind gusts in the 35 to 47 mph range were observed by weather stations in Tazewell and Richlands, both in Tazewell county.  These non-thunderstorm related wind gusts were responsible for blowing down at least seven trees across the county, including one tree that fell on and caused damage to three cars parked at the Cedar Bluff Post Office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts measured by a mesonet station in Tazewell and the AWOS at Richlands showed wind gusts of 40 and 47 mph, respectively in the 12:30 pm to 3:30 pm EST time frame.  At least seven trees were down across the county as a result of these strong winds.  One tree fell on three parked cars at the Cedar Bluff Post Office around 2:35 pm EST causing damage to the vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough, developing through the central U.S. and swinging into the southeast states the Tuesday before Thanksgiving induced an area of low pressure along the eastern Gulf Coastal region.  As the upper trough shifted into the eastern U.S., the surface low underwent explosive development as it moved northward along the southeast and Mid-Atlantic coastal region.  The deepening surface low pulled cold air and moisture into the region bringing a period of snow to areas west of the Blue Ridge on one of the busiest travel days of the year.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the one to three inch range, but several locations saw snowfall in the three to four inch range across southwest Virginia, northwest North Carolina, and southeast West Virginia.  The heaviest snowfall within southwest Virginia was at Mount Rogers in Grayson county where eight inches of snow was observed.  The heavy wet snow resulted and combined gusty winds resulted in power outages for several thousands residents of southwest Virginia counties.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of mid-morning on the day before Thanksgiving, namely November 26, 2014. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation evolved Tuesday as a cold front stalled just to the south of the Mid-Atlantic region and high pressure built in over New England. This synoptic set up resulted in persisting northeasterly flow that kept surface temperatures at or just below freezing while southwesterly flow in the lower levels provided a deep warm layer. EVENT_NARRATIVE: OKV reported visibilities of one-quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front sweep through far western Virginia between 0200E and 0400E on Monday the 6th.   Rain showers and temperatures in the 40s and 50s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by dawn.  Snow accumulations were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down into the single digits by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th were mostly 5 below zero to 10 below zero.  The coldest temperatures came from the highest elevations. ||Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 30 degree range overnight and into the morning hours for most counties.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to recover during the day on the 7th.  However, the wind did subside during the afternoon.  ||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, home furnace difficulties, and  vehicular engine problems occurred.  Repair companies were kept busy.   County public school systems were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building in from the west coupled with a deepening upper level trough ushered in record breaking cold temperatures for multiple days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of temperatures and winds resulted in a wind chill of at least -10oF during the early morning hours on the 18th and 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very high amplitude upper atmospheric pattern featured equally deep troughs in the western and eastern U.S.  Within the eastern U.S. trough, was a vigorous Alberta clipper that intensified immensely as it plunged into the southeast states into the base of the upper trough.  Meanwhile...a large nearly 1040mb Canadian High was plunging into the central and eastern U.S. on the back side of the developing storm system in the southeast states.  The combination of these features brought an early season snowfall principally to the Appalachian mountains of eastern Tennessee, far southwest Virginia, and northwest North Carolina.  Light snow also fell in the mountains of eastern West Virginia, but the storm system tracked too far south for any significant amounts in that region.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range across this region...with some higher totals in the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina.  Strong and gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow in these areas causing scattered power outages.  A few locations recorded wind gusts in excess of 45 mph, meeting strong wind criteria.  These included Hot Springs in Bath county with a wind gust of 46 mph at 8:35 am EST on 11/2/14, the AWOS at the Galax/Hillsville Airport on 11/2/14 at 12:35 am EST, and a weather station located four miles west of Dublin in Pulaski county which recorded a wind gust of 47 mph on 11/2/14 at 4:05 am EST.  Several hundred residents of Tazewell county in far southwest Virginia were left without power for several hours from the comination of 30 to 40 mph wind gusts and the heavy, wet snow.  Because of warm temperatures within the preceding days and the early season time of the event, roads and travel conditions were not significantly impacted.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of the afternoon of November 1, 2014.  These denote the high end of the event in these counties.  Note, the event actually began during the afternoon of October 31st.||Bland (Ceres) - 1 inch,|Carroll (6N Galax, Woodlawn) - 3 inches,|City of Galax - 4 inches,|Floyd (Floyd) - 3 inches,|Giles (Mountain Lake) - 3 inches,|Grayson (5NE Independence) - 4 inches,|Patrick (Meadows of Dan) - 4 inches,|Pulaski (2S Snowville) - 2 inches,|Smyth (Marion) - 2 inches,|Tazewell (Tazewell) - 4 inches,|Wythe (Rural Retreat) - 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed four inches of snow at Meadows-of-Dan at an elevation over 3000 feet, with 1/2 to 2.5 inches of snow south of Meadows-of-Dan and south of Floyd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colder air started to filter in on Monday the 27th.  Yet, a storm across the southeastern states brushed the area with an inch or less of snow during the late day and evening on the 28th.||In its wake, drier and colder air moved south for dawn on Wednesday the 29th.  Yet, the main force of this cold wave remained further north, including Ohio and West Virginia.   Temperatures were slightly below zero for most communities.  Clintwood had a minimum temperature of 6 below zero for one of the colder readings.  ||A second cold night was felt with dawn temperatures on the 30th with very similar temperatures, mostly in the zero to 5 below zero range.  However, Clintwood again observed 6 below zero.    ||The environment began to moderate on Thursday afternoon the 30th.

A deep upper trough, developing through the central U.S. and swinging into the southeast states the Tuesday before Thanksgiving induced an area of low pressure along the eastern Gulf Coastal region.  As the upper trough shifted into the eastern U.S., the surface low underwent explosive development as it moved northward along the southeast and Mid-Atlantic coastal region.  The deepening surface low pulled cold air and moisture into the region bringing a period of snow to areas west of the Blue Ridge on one of the busiest travel days of the year.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the one to three inch range, but several locations saw snowfall in the three to four inch range across southwest Virginia, northwest North Carolina, and southeast West Virginia.  The heaviest snowfall within southwest Virginia was at Mount Rogers in Grayson county where eight inches of snow was observed.  The heavy wet snow resulted and combined gusty winds resulted in power outages for several thousands residents of southwest Virginia counties.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of mid-morning on the day before Thanksgiving, namely November 26, 2014.  The amounts below represent the maximum amounts reported from these counties.||Alleghany (4E Covington) - 3 inches,|Bath (Warm Springs) - 4 inches,|Bedford (peaks of Otter) - 4 inches,|Bland (5N Bland) - 3 inches,|Botetourt (Fincastle) - 1.5 inch,|Carroll (Hebron) - 4.9 inches,|Craig (2W Simmonsville) - 6.5 inches,|Floyd (Check) - 4.5 inches,|Giles (Mountain Lake) - 5 inches,|Grayson (Mount Rogers) - 8 inches,|Montgomery (2SSW Christiansburg) - 5.8 inches,|Patrick (Meadows of Dan) - 3.8 inches,|Pulaski (2S Snowville) - 6.1 inches,|Roanoke (3ESE Cave Spring) - 2.5 inches,|Rockbridge (Raphine) - 4.0 inches,|Smyth (Sugar Grove) - 3.5 inches,|Tazewell (Burkes Garden) - 1.6 inch,|Wythe (Rural Retreat) - 3.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported 5.8 inches of snow just southwest of Christiansburg while a trained spotter at Laurel Ridge observed 5.8 inches of snow as well.  Elsewhere around the county, snowfall ranged from 1 to 2 inches in the Blacksburg area, to 3.5 to 4.5 inches on the north side of Blacksburg on Brush Mountain and also in the Alleghany Springs area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was lifting northward through the southeast and southern Mid-Atlantic states in advance of a strong cold front and intensifying upper trough.  This pattern allowed unseasonably warm air from the Gulf of Mexico to be brought northward into the region in advance of the front.  Widespread heavy rain showers and even a few thunderstorms spread into the region during the afternoon and evening.  However, one area of thunderstorms across the southern Shenandoah Valley became severe during the late evening hours producing wind damage in eastern Rockbridge county, especially near Buena Vista, where several trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockbridge County 911 Center reported that a tree was down on U.S. Route 663 or Tye River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance combining with northwest winds triggered upslope snow showers on the western side of the Potomac Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported a 12 hour storm snowfall total of 4.5 inches near Hightown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very high amplitude upper atmospheric pattern featured equally deep troughs in the western and eastern U.S.  Within the eastern U.S. trough, was a vigorous Alberta clipper that intensified immensely as it plunged into the southeast states into the base of the upper trough.  Meanwhile...a large nearly 1040mb Canadian High was plunging into the central and eastern U.S. on the back side of the developing storm system in the southeast states.  The combination of these features brought an early season snowfall principally to the Appalachian mountains of eastern Tennessee, far southwest Virginia, and northwest North Carolina.  Light snow also fell in the mountains of eastern West Virginia, but the storm system tracked too far south for any significant amounts in that region.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range across this region...with some higher totals in the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina.  Strong and gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow in these areas causing scattered power outages.  A few locations recorded wind gusts in excess of 45 mph, meeting strong wind criteria.  These included Hot Springs in Bath county with a wind gust of 46 mph at 8:35 am EST on 11/2/14, the AWOS at the Galax/Hillsville Airport on 11/2/14 at 12:35 am EST, and a weather station located four miles west of Dublin in Pulaski county which recorded a wind gust of 47 mph on 11/2/14 at 4:05 am EST.  Several hundred residents of Tazewell county in far southwest Virginia were left without power for several hours from the comination of 30 to 40 mph wind gusts and the heavy, wet snow.  Because of warm temperatures within the preceding days and the early season time of the event, roads and travel conditions were not significantly impacted.||Here are some of the snow reports from southwest Virginia as of the afternoon of November 1, 2014.  These denote the high end of the event in these counties.  Note, the event actually began during the afternoon of October 31st.||Bland (Ceres) - 1 inch,|Carroll (6N Galax, Woodlawn) - 3 inches,|City of Galax - 4 inches,|Floyd (Floyd) - 3 inches,|Giles (Mountain Lake) - 3 inches,|Grayson (5NE Independence) - 4 inches,|Patrick (Meadows of Dan) - 4 inches,|Pulaski (2S Snowville) - 2 inches,|Smyth (Marion) - 2 inches,|Tazewell (Tazewell) - 4 inches,|Wythe (Rural Retreat) - 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bland County Sheriff's Office reported from 1/2 inch of snow at Long Spur and Bland to 1 inch at Ceres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moving east off the Northeast Coast set up a weak cold air damming scenario as low pressure moved along a stalled frontal boundary from the Gulf of Mexico to off the coast of the Carolinas.  Also, a secondary area of weak low pressure moving across southern Virginia intensified as it coupled with a mid-level wave moving east out of Tennessee Valley. Light snow fell across inland Virgina with the heaviest snowfall occurring across the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore and Virginia Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 0.4 inches was reported 5 mile west of Glen Allen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation as high pressure retreated to the north of the area and low pressure formed over the Mid-Atlantic region. The northeast flow kept temperatures right around freezing during the evening and overnight hours while the forming low brought plenty of moisture, resulting in on-going precipitation for most of the night and into the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of ice accumulations between a hundredth and two-tenths of an inch were reported around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped develop snow after dawn on the 25th.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Snow totals of 2 to 4 inches were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass slipped into the Southern Appalachian region followed by a strong upper level system that pushed east from the Lower Mississippi Valley.  Sufficient moisture was available for lifting over the dense and cold surface air resulting in an ice storm that produced accretions ranging from around one tenth to as much as one half inch. The area affected extended from the Cumberland Plateau counties closer to the Kentucky border east northeast across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accretion across Wise County ranged from one tenth to as much as one half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dome of Canadian high pressure built into the mid-Atlantic region behind an Alberta Clipper cold front. The very tight pressure gradient between the two systems resulted in very gusty northwest winds across the mountains, as well as a period of upslope snow showers that were heavy at times.

A wedge cold front was stalled across southwest Virginia into north central North Carolina.  Temperatures behind the wedge front were well below seasonal normals with maximum temperatures only in the 50s.  Meanwhile...a very deep closed upper low (467mb at 500mb) was shifting slowing eastward from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A deep conveyor belt of moisture was spreading northward from the Gulf of Mexico up and over the shallow wedge of cool air.  Light to moderate rain had persisted across the region for a couple of days, but rainfall amounts had been mostly around 1 to 1.5 inch. However, as the wedge boundary began to retreat during the afternoon, strong thunderstorms with very heavy rain formed across Southside Virginia and the Virginia and North Carolina Piedmont and spread across the region in several bands, dumping local rainfall amounts in excess of 2.0 inches in a few hours.  This result in localized flash flooding mainly across Charlotte and Campbell counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WDBJ7 Television of Roanoke reported that a low water crossing bridge over the Roanoke River on Wiley Road, just south of the heart of Roanoke City, was closed due to flood waters from the Roanoke River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passed up the Atlantic coast, radiating moisture inland which was forced into the mountains.  Canadian high pressure wedged along the eastern face of the Appalachian chain kept temperatures cold enough that the moisture precipitated in the form of snow across the mid-Atlantic region.  Snowfall amounts across the region ranged mainly between 1 and 3 inches, with 4 inches observed in a few areas. In many areas, especially those east of the Blue Ridge, this snowfall amounted to the first significant snow accumulations of the winter season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slick road conditions from snowfall and icing resulted in several automobile accidents across the Southside region of Virginia.  Most were minor accidents with no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system affecting the Atlantic coast was followed by a strong arctic cold front passing across the region during the early morning hours of January 3rd, resulting in brief but heavy accumulating snow confined mainly to the west-facing slopes from southeast West Virginia through the mountains of North Carolina.  Winds with the arctic surge topped 60 mph in a few places across the mountains and, when combined with snow, resulted in periods of localized blizzard-like conditions.  Temperatures dropped into the teens and the positive single digits across the mountains, resulting flash-freezing of wet surfaces from rainfall on January 2nd.  Wind chills across the mountains were exceptionally cold, dropping into the negative teens.  Gradient winds began to relax by sunrise on the 3rd, allowing snow shower activity and blowing snow to diminish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blow down across Carroll County during the pre-dawn hours of January 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system affecting the Atlantic coast was followed by a strong arctic cold front passing across the region during the early morning hours of January 3rd, resulting in brief but heavy accumulating snow confined mainly to the west-facing slopes from southeast West Virginia through the mountains of North Carolina.  Winds with the arctic surge topped 60 mph in a few places across the mountains and, when combined with snow, resulted in periods of localized blizzard-like conditions.  Temperatures dropped into the teens and the positive single digits across the mountains, resulting flash-freezing of wet surfaces from rainfall on January 2nd.  Wind chills across the mountains were exceptionally cold, dropping into the negative teens.  Gradient winds began to relax by sunrise on the 3rd, allowing snow shower activity and blowing snow to diminish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill of -23F was observed at the Hot Springs, VA AWOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of arctic air arrived into the mid-Atlantic during the early morning hours of January 6th. Temperatures continued to fall over the next 24 hours, eventually bottoming out below zero nearly everywhere west of the Blue Ridge.  Winds remained gusty through that 24 hour period as well due to a tightened pressure gradient as high pressure built into the region. The result was dangerously low wind chill temperatures not see in the region in many years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures were observed in the -20F to -22F range at several locations throughout the county during the early morning hours of January 7th, 2014.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official gage at Lynchburg Airport (LYH) measured 3.88 inches of rain in about 2.5 hours. It was the 2nd wettest daily total for this date and also 2nd for any July day with records dating back to 1893. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts up to 52 knots (60 miles per hour) produced widespead damage across the City of Chesapeake. The most significant damage occurred near the Chesapeake Regional Medical Center in Greenbrier where numerous trees were downed. Roof damage was also sustained by four houses along Nottaway Drive. An airport hanger at the Chesapeake Regional Airport was also destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge of high pressure aloft was centered along the south central Gulf Coastal region.  Some of the hottest weather of the summer season was present across the area at the time with maximum temperatures into the low 90s across the Piedmont, with 80s across the mountains. An upper-level disturbance was tracking through the Great Lakes into the northeastern United States.  This disturbance impinged upon the hot, humid, unstable air mass in place across the region to generate scattered to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these became severe in the northwest part of the forecast area, particularly in far southeastern West Virginia and adjacent areas of southwest Virginia, producing damaging wind gusts and marginally severe hail along with frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Campbell County 911 Center reported that a tree was down blocking Bethany Road, approximately one mile east-southeast of Rustburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms became severe with damage producing wind gusts during the afternoon ahead of a cold front as steep lapse rates, high instability and fairly strong winds aloft contributed to organizing the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cable line was reported down along I-64 at mile marker 35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly pushed across the area during the afternoon and evening and interacted with deep moisture from Hurricane Arthur tracking up the east coast. This triggered widespread thunderstorms activity with some high winds, hail and heavy rainfall. A Flash Flood Watch was issued early on the 3rd and showers and thunderstorms by mid-afternoon prompted the issuance of several Flood Advisories (FLS) mainly in urban areas including Roanoke and Buena Vista. A Small Stream FLS was issued by late afternoon across parts of Wythe and Grayson counties where radar estimated over 3 inches of rainfall in some basins.  An Urban Flood Warning was issued for the City of Galax, where there were reports of some urban-type flooding.  As the area of heavy rainfall shifted further east a Flash Flood Warning (FFW) was issued for parts of Patrick, Henry, Floyd and Franklin counties at 513 PM. Later information was received that a road was completely washed out by flood waters in eastern Floyd County in an area of 2 to 4 inch rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shooting Creek Road was damaged by runoff from heavy rains. A VDOT crew was repairing the road the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a warm front that was draped from southern Ohio across the southern Appalachian region. A couple of the storms across southwest Virginia produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a total of five trees down and brought down the top of another.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly pushed across the area during the afternoon and evening and interacted with deep moisture from Hurricane Arthur tracking up the east coast. This triggered widespread thunderstorms activity with some high winds, hail and heavy rainfall. A Flash Flood Watch was issued early on the 3rd and showers and thunderstorms by mid-afternoon prompted the issuance of several Flood Advisories (FLS) mainly in urban areas including Roanoke and Buena Vista. A Small Stream FLS was issued by late afternoon across parts of Wythe and Grayson counties where radar estimated over 3 inches of rainfall in some basins.  An Urban Flood Warning was issued for the City of Galax, where there were reports of some urban-type flooding.  As the area of heavy rainfall shifted further east a Flash Flood Warning (FFW) was issued for parts of Patrick, Henry, Floyd and Franklin counties at 513 PM. Later information was received that a road was completely washed out by flood waters in eastern Floyd County in an area of 2 to 4 inch rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds on Gala Loop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms became severe with damage producing wind gusts during the afternoon ahead of a cold front as steep lapse rates, high instability and fairly strong winds aloft contributed to organizing the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down by thunderstorm winds at a residence along Route 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the Mid-Atlantic while a stationary boundary was across the region. Warm and moist conditions led to a unstable environment and showers and thunderstorms moved across the region. Some storms led to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down on the intersection of Lahore Road and Brick Church Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down along U.S. Route 58 east of Stuart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding across southern portions of the Virginia Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in a small area around Cape Charles, VA. Flooding was reported on many streets in Cape Charles. Several cars were flooded by 2 to 3 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was observed passing north along the Appalachian Mountains, while surface high pressure was observed wedging southward from New England across the mid-Atlantic region. The upper trough trigger a band of locally heavy rain with a few embedded thunderstorms.  Despite the presence of the high pressure wedge, strong downdraft winds were able to reach the surface, producing localized damage to a tree.  Wind gusts at nearby weather observing stations reported 25 mph to 30 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally strong wind gusts blew off the top half of a large hardwood tree in community of Ringgold, VA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the northwest pushed into a typically very warm and humid air mass, with thunderstorms resulting. A few storms became severe producing pockets of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five trees were reported blown down in the Rocky Mount area, including one on Ball Park Road, one on Doe Run Road, one on Level Valley Road, one on Franklin Turnpike, and another on Claiborne Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved south the Mid-Atlantic and low level moisture resulted in dense fog formation in the Northern Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of a quarter mile or less were measured at Luray Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glass porch was blown out. Wind gust of 52 knots (60 mph) was estimated at NWS Wakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached the region, surface winds increased significantly in advance of the front on Tuesday the 14th, and then continued very gusty behind the front into the early morning hours of Wednesday the 15th. A large number of trees were blown down in association with these winds. Showers and storms developed in advance of the front and also occurred coincident to the passage of the front. Trees were blown down in association with a few of the storms increasing to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew several utility poles and trees and limbs down in and around the Rocky Gap area. Damage values estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south southeast winds followed by strong west winds in advance of and behind a cold front resulted in moderate coastal flooding across portions of the Virginia Northern Neck adjacent to the Chesapeake Bay and the Tidal Potomac River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal anomalies between 1.5 feet to around 2.0 feet above normal produced moderate flooding along portions of the Virginia Northern Neck adjacent to the Chesapeake Bay and the Tidal Potomac River. Lewisetta recorded a maximum storm tide of 3.51 feet MLLW at 954 pm October 3rd, and 3.78 feet MLLW at 1030 am October 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved into the Mid Atlantic and mid level forcing led to snow across portions of the Mid Atlantic. Wrap around moisture around the surface low and dynamics from the upper low led to snow fall accumulation across Washington DC metro and Southern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 2 inches or more measured at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up the east coast and approached the Mid Atlantic. High pressure was located across New England and fed cold air into the region. Heavy snow fell across most parts of the Mid Atlantic with the highest amounts near the Mason Dixon line where mid level forcing led to a heavy band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 or more inches were measured at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet over parts of the western Virginia piedmont. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid to late afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along parts of the New River and upper Roanoke valleys.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches along and east of a Martinsville to Lynchburg line, 10 to 14 inches across the Mountain Empire part of southwest Virginia, 12 to 16 inches just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge and north into the southern Shenandoah valley, with 16 to 26 inches in an area between Covington Virginia south into the Blacksburg to Roanoke region and farther south to near Galax. The highest end of this range was centered over Floyd County.||Sleet amounts were generally less than an inch between Martinsville and Danville. Freezing rain occurred mainly along and east of a line from Martinsville to Buckingham. Amounts ranged from around one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 inches across the eastern part of the county to 11 inches in the west. Some light sleet also occurred with amounts around one tenth of an inch. Freezing rain accretion produced one to two tenths of an inch across western and eastern part of the county. Central areas received a little over one-quarter of an inch of ice. Two vehicles were involved in a head-on collision on snow covered roads resulting in indirect damage to the two vehicles and one indirect fatality of the 55 year old male driver of one of the vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 or more inches were measured at Haywood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet over parts of the western Virginia piedmont. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid to late afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along parts of the New River and upper Roanoke valleys.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches along and east of a Martinsville to Lynchburg line, 10 to 14 inches across the Mountain Empire part of southwest Virginia, 12 to 16 inches just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge and north into the southern Shenandoah valley, with 16 to 26 inches in an area between Covington Virginia south into the Blacksburg to Roanoke region and farther south to near Galax. The highest end of this range was centered over Floyd County.||Sleet amounts were generally less than an inch between Martinsville and Danville. Freezing rain occurred mainly along and east of a line from Martinsville to Buckingham. Amounts ranged from around one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from around 10 inches across the eastern part of the county to 15 inches in the west. Some light sleet also occurred with amounts generally less than one-half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet over parts of the western Virginia piedmont. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid to late afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along parts of the New River and upper Roanoke valleys.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches along and east of a Martinsville to Lynchburg line, 10 to 14 inches across the Mountain Empire part of southwest Virginia, 12 to 16 inches just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge and north into the southern Shenandoah valley, with 16 to 26 inches in an area between Covington Virginia south into the Blacksburg to Roanoke region and farther south to near Galax. The highest end of this range was centered over Floyd County.||Sleet amounts were generally less than an inch between Martinsville and Danville. Freezing rain occurred mainly along and east of a line from Martinsville to Buckingham. Amounts ranged from around one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from around 9 inches in the western part of the county to 17 inches in the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracking northeast across the central Appalachians combined with energy diving in from the northwest to result in a deep upper low that slowly moved east of the Blue Ridge to off the Mid-Atlantic coast. The system was preceded by shallow convective shower bands before the trailing strong cold front punched across the mountains in the early morning hours resulting in bursts of very strong north to northwest winds which topped 65 mph in spots along the southern Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down due to high winds near the Pines Campground along Virginia State Route 617.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet over parts of the western Virginia piedmont. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid to late afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along parts of the New River and upper Roanoke valleys.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches along and east of a Martinsville to Lynchburg line, 10 to 14 inches across the Mountain Empire part of southwest Virginia, 12 to 16 inches just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge and north into the southern Shenandoah valley, with 16 to 26 inches in an area between Covington Virginia south into the Blacksburg to Roanoke region and farther south to near Galax. The highest end of this range was centered over Floyd County.||Sleet amounts were generally less than an inch between Martinsville and Danville. Freezing rain occurred mainly along and east of a line from Martinsville to Buckingham. Amounts ranged from around one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 9 to 11 inches across most of the county. Near Galax, amounts were closer to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracking northeast across the central Appalachians combined with energy diving in from the northwest to result in a deep upper low that slowly moved east of the Blue Ridge to off the Mid-Atlantic coast. The system was preceded by shallow convective shower bands before the trailing strong cold front punched across the mountains in the early morning hours resulting in bursts of very strong north to northwest winds which topped 65 mph in spots along the southern Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of trees that were blown down in Salem also brought down some power lines along Central Avenue. The peak wind gust at Roanoke Airport ASOS (KROA) was 50 mph at around 9 PM (EST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wedge cold front was stalled across southwest Virginia into north central North Carolina.  Temperatures behind the wedge front were well below seasonal normals with maximum temperatures only in the 50s.  Meanwhile...a very deep closed upper low (467mb at 500mb) was shifting slowing eastward from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A deep conveyor belt of moisture was spreading northward from the Gulf of Mexico up and over the shallow wedge of cool air.  Light to moderate rain had persisted across the region for a couple of days, but rainfall amounts had been mostly around 1 to 1.5 inch. However, as the wedge boundary began to retreat during the afternoon, strong thunderstorms with very heavy rain formed across Southside Virginia and the Virginia and North Carolina Piedmont and spread across the region in several bands, dumping local rainfall amounts in excess of 2.0 inches in a few hours.  This result in localized flash flooding mainly across Charlotte and Campbell counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Charlotte County 911 Center reported that Robin Road was flooded and closed approximately six miles east-northeast of Brookneal where it crosses Turnip Creek as a result of flood waters from Turnip Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front stretched from east Tennessee eastward through North Carolina.  A strong cold front, supported by a vigorous upper-level trough was approaching from the west.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along the warm front across the Piedmont in the warm, unstable, somewhat sheared environment.  Several of the storms became strong and a few severe producing pea to quarter-sized hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lynchburg Television Media reported quarter-sized hail fives miles southeast of Halifax.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern half of the United States was under cyclonic flow and moisture from the Atlantic and Gulf continued to move into the Mid Atlantic. A warm front moved northward and showers and thunderstorms broke out across the area. Heavy rain produced flash flooding and rapid rises on streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Accotink Creek at Annandale exceeded its flood stage of 9.5 feet. Nearby roads were flooded. Woodburn Road and Prosperity Avenue were flooded at Accotink Creek. Water reached an outbuilding near Braddock Road and covered ballields at Wakefield Park. This event peakted at 12.52 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating served to destabilize the atmosphere during the afternoon and evening, while weak surface convergence associated with a surface trough along the lee side of the Blue Ridge Mountains.  Very little upper level support was available as thunderstorms developed, keeping the coverage of shower and thunderstorm activity isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was observed at the intersection of Riverside Road and Piney Forest Road.

Temperatures behind the wedge front were well below seasonal normals with maximum temperatures only in the 50s.  Meanwhile...a very deep closed upper low (467mb at 500mb) was shifting slowing eastward from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A deep conveyor belt of moisture was spreading northward from the Gulf of Mexico up and over the shallow wedge of cool air. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved north during the overnight hours of the 11th into the 12th.  Rains estimated at 1.5 to 2 inches of fell in a few hours over wet terrain.  Minor flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Feeder streams into Knox Creek, including Laurel Fork, flooded and closed roads.  No structures were flooded.  Mud and debris slides also occurred in this steep terrain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported thunderstorms produced golfball size hail 5 miles west of Nickelsville  in Midway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep trough was tracking through the eastern United States accompanied by a strong cold front and a large unseasonably cold area of Canadian air on the 15th.  Strong gradient westerly winds behind the cold front caused wind gusts over 60 MPH along Interstate 77 in southwest Virginia near Fancy Gap and the Virginia/North Carolina border.  This area is well noted for intense winds in these type of synoptic situations as wind channels along the interstate through the gap in the 2000 to 3000 foot mountains in this region of Virginia.  The strong winds caused damage to homes near Fancy Gap and also blew two 18-wheel tractor trailers over on Interstate 77 just before the 3-mile marker on Interstate 77 North in Carroll County Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Virginia State Police and Virginia Department of Transportation (VDOT) Officials reported that an 18-wheel tractor trailer loaded with 8000 pounds of plastic bottles was blown over on its side on Interstate 77 North just before mile-marker 3.0.  While VDOT was attempting to erect the tractor-trailer, another 18-wheel tractor-trailer truck overturned on the VDOT vehicle.  While trying to erect this second tractor-trailer, the strong winds sustained at times near 50 knots, moved the trailer 30 feet and into the side of the Virginia State Police vehicle causing at least $1500 in damages to the police vehicle alone.  Despite all of the damage, no injuries or fatalities were reported.  Interstate 77 was closed for nearly seven hours during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely-scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong cold front making its way across the Appalachian chain during the evening of May 10th. The strongest thunderstorm activity occurred as an upper level disturbance passed across the forecast region during the early afternoon, however limited instability prevented most of the thunderstorms from reaching severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the mountains as a cold front entered the region from the north.  While instability was not impressive ahead of the front, it was sufficient enough to allow a single thunderstorm to become severe as it passed southeast from the mountains of southern Virginia to the North Carolina state line before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One-inch diameter hail was observed on Holbert Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system passed east across the northern Great Plains, drawing deep moisture along the Appalachian Chain from the Gulf of Mexico.  An upper level disturbance moved across the central Appalachians early in the day, pushing a line of showers with heavy rain across the forecast region.  While a break in rainfall developed across the mountains and foothills behind the line of convection, the line stalled east of Danville and Lynchburg, where prolonged periods of heavy rain fell throughout the afternoon.  A cold front entered the Mountain Empire and southeast West Virginia during mid-afternoon, triggering another round of heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms that quickly filled in ahead of the boundary.  For the entire event, one to three inch rainfall amounts were common across the entire forecast region.  ||In addition to some reports of flash flooding, river flooding was also observed from Halifax through Buckingham Counties. An upper level disturbance moved across the central Appalachians early in the day, pushing a line of showers with heavy rain across the forecast region.  While a break in rainfall developed across the mountains and foothills behind the line of convection, the line stalled east of Danville and Lynchburg, where prolonged periods of heavy rain fell throughout the afternoon.  A cold front entered the Mountain Empire and southeast West Virginia during mid-afternoon, triggering another round of heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms that quickly filled in ahead of the boundary.  For the entire event, one to three inch rainfall amounts were common across the entire forecast region.  ||In addition to some reports of flash flooding, river flooding was also observed from Halifax through Buckingham Counties.  The Roanoke River at Randolph was among those most affected, with the river rising to the moderate flood stage of 24.9 feet.||Below are locations which received at least three inches of rainfall for the event and their total rainfall amounts.||North Carolina|...Caswell County...|    4.42   Yanceyville 4 SE COOP||...Watauga County...|    4.19   Sandy Flats IFLOWS|    4.18   2 NE blowing Rock CCR|    3.92   1 WSW Foscoe CCR|    3.75   Blackburn Knob IFLOWS|    3.70   Boone 1 SE COOP|    3.60   Howards Knob IFLOWS|    3.47   Boone|    3.47   Foscoe IFLOWS|    3.20   Parkway School IFLOWS|    3.11   Triplett ||Virginia|...Appomattox County...|    3.53   Appomattox COOP||...Buckingham County...|    3.84   Buckingham COOP||...Campbell County...|    3.68   3 E Brookneal CCR|    3.44   Brookneal COOP|    3.24   1 WSW Lynchburg CCR|    3.20   Lynchburg Airport|    3.12   Concord||...Charlotte County...|    5.68   4 SE Saxe CCR|    4.98   Charlotte Courthouse COOP||...Floyd County...|    3.03   6 S Copper Hill CCR||...Halifax County...|    4.43   Randolph|    3.74   South Boston COOP||...Tazewell County...|    3.25   Springville IFLOWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary was stalled from Tennessee to northern Florida, providing low level convergence to trigger isolated thunderstorm activity.  Daytime heating provided enough instability to support a few severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Mica Road.  Another tree was blown down on Morehead Avenue, blocking one lane of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eastern half of the United States was under cyclonic flow and moisture from the Atlantic and Gulf continued to move into the Mid Atlantic. A warm front moved northward and showers and thunderstorms broke out across the area. Heavy rain produced flash flooding and rapid rises on streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Robinson River stream gauge at Locust Dale exceeded its flood stage of 11 feet. This event peaked at 14.01 feet. At 14 ft, multiple spots on Route 721 are flooded, along with other nearby low lying land. Access may be affected for residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary was stalled from Tennessee to northern Florida, providing low level convergence to trigger isolated thunderstorm activity.  Daytime heating provided enough instability to support a few severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down, blocking one lane of Soapstone Road.  Another tree was blown down near the intersection of Joseph Martin Highway and Fisher Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating served to destabilize the atmosphere during the afternoon and evening, while weak surface convergence associated with a surface trough along the lee side of the Blue Ridge Mountains.  Very little upper level support was available as thunderstorms developed, keeping the coverage of shower and thunderstorm activity isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike at the Peak Creek substation behind the Virginia Eagle Distributing Company occurred at 2:22 p.m., shutting down electricity to about 8,100 Appalachian Power Company customers in the town of Pulaski and Pulaski County.  Power was restored around 4 p.m.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on Route 602.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front stretched from east Tennessee eastward through North Carolina.  A strong cold front, supported by a vigorous upper-level trough was approaching from the west.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along the warm front across the Piedmont in the warm, unstable, somewhat sheared environment.  Several of the storms became strong and a few severe producing pea to quarter-sized hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Halifax County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds on Calvary Road approximately five miles northeast of Milton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of nickel-sized hail were received from a trained spotter and the public from five miles south of South Boston, to three miles east-southeast of South Boston, to three miles east-northeast of South Boston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front approached the region from southern New England, providing the low level convergence needed to trigger shower and thunderstorm activity.  Strong daytime heating provided decent instability, with CAPE values ranging from 1000 to 2000 J/kg during late afternoon.  Weak upper level dynamics however kept shower and thunderstorm isolated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado developed just north of Highway 460 and south of Petersburg Road approximately one mile northwest of Waverly. The tornado moved south crossing Highway 460 just north of Waverly. As the tornado tracked across Highway 460, it struck the NAPA auto parts store causing only minor damage. Numerous large trees were uprooted and snapped along Highway 460. Highway 460 was closed for a time due to the downed trees. The tornado continued tracking south to North Church Street causing minor damage to the First Baptist Church. Numerous large trees fell into the neighboring cemetery damaging a number of sites. Continuing south, the tornado tracked along New Street snapping trees and causing minor damage to homes breaking out windows and removing siding. The tornado lifted shortly after crossing Highway 40 on the west side of Waverly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged south into southern West Virginia by dawn on the 4th.  A strong low pressure for early June moved east through the Midwest, along the frontal boundary, during the day on the 4th.  Dew points were in the upper 60s and lower 70s near and south of the front.  The disturbance passed to the east by early on the 5th. ||Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms passed through eastern Kentucky and West Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours on the 4th.   The last round of the showers and thunderstorms sank down into northern Buchanan County after 0000E on the 5th.  These lingering bands of showers and storms became oriented northwest to southeast along their movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 to 3.5 inches of rain fell in less than 3 hours.  The hardest hit basins in this steep mountain terrain were along Upper Elk Creek and Guesses Fork.  Small private bridges to homes were washed out.  Roads were damaged.  A few homes had minor basement flooding.||The axis of this small heavy rain area crossed the state border.  Flash flooding also occurred in a small section of McDowell County, West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level storm system impacted the region on Sunday, July 27th, bringing severe weather along with locally heavy rainfall. There were several reports of straight-line wind damage and golf ball to baseball sized hail. A storm system of this magnitude is highly unusual for this time of year due to the strength of the front and the record cool temperatures it produced in the middle of the Summer season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in Duffield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary triggered thunderstorms during the afternoon for a second day in a row across southwest Virgina.  Trees and powerlines were downed by the storm.  One 56 year man in Abingdon was killed when a tree fell while his was walking.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media personnel reported a pedestrian was struck by a falling tree in the 100 block of West Main street in Abingdon.  The victim was a 56 year old at the time of the event.  At the time of the event the peak wind gusts were only recorded at 24 mph via the CWOP station 1 mile south of Abingdon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple, heavy rain producing thunderstorms moved across portions of southwest Virginia during the afternoon hours. Some locations received generous rainfall and heavy rainfall rates on the order of 4 to 6 inches per hour. The community of Chilhowie receive such a storm that produced flash flooding that closed roads and damaged at least one building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm produce up to three inches of rain. This amount of water caused flash flooding that prompted street closures along and near the main thoroughfare of the Lee Highway/Route 11/Main Steet. Specific locations closed include Route 11 near Walton Avenue, the intersection of Deer Valley and Taddler Branch, Route 11 at Pine Avenue, and portions of Hood Street. A business was inundated with flood waters along east Main Street. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dome of Canadian high pressure built into the mid-Atlantic region behind an Alberta Clipper cold front. The very tight pressure gradient between the two systems resulted in very gusty northwest winds across the mountains, as well as a period of upslope snow showers that were heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranging between 1 and 4 inches, as well as roadway icing, resulted in slick roads that caused several automobile accidents across western Virginia.  Most accidents were minor, but one resulted in an injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system crossed through Ohio into southern Pennsylvania during the daylight hours of the 12th.  Temperatures were in the 50s and 60s ahead of its associated cold front.  The cold front swept through far western Virginia during the afternoon hours.  In the wake of the front, falling temperatures and strong pressure rises resulted in widespread wind gusts around 45 mph.  A fallen tree damaged a house in Oakwood.  In the same area, a tree fell onto a truck.||In the colder air overnight into the 13th, banded snow showers fell.  A few enhanced streaks had accumulations of  2 to 3 inches of snow.  For example, the cooperative observer measured 3 inches.  The county schools were closed in Dickenson County, while Buchanan County had a 2 hour delay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet over parts of the western Virginia piedmont. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid to late afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along parts of the New River and upper Roanoke valleys.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches along and east of a Martinsville to Lynchburg line, 10 to 14 inches across the Mountain Empire part of southwest Virginia, 12 to 16 inches just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge and north into the southern Shenandoah valley, with 16 to 26 inches in an area between Covington Virginia south into the Blacksburg to Roanoke region and farther south to near Galax. The highest end of this range was centered over Floyd County.||Sleet amounts were generally less than an inch between Martinsville and Danville. Freezing rain occurred mainly along and east of a line from Martinsville to Buckingham. Amounts ranged from around one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from around 9 inches across the eastern part of the county near Lynchburg to 19 inches across the western part near Roanoke. Amounts were around 14 inches in Bedford. A total of 50 vehicle accidents were indirectly caused by snow covered roads. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system crossed through Ohio into southern Pennsylvania during the daylight hours of the 12th.  Temperatures were in the 50s and 60s ahead of its associated cold front.  The cold front swept through far western Virginia during the afternoon hours.  In the wake of the front, falling temperatures and strong pressure rises resulted in widespread wind gusts around 45 mph.  A fallen tree damaged a house in Oakwood.  In the same area, a tree fell onto a truck.||In the colder air overnight into the 13th, banded snow showers fell.  A few enhanced streaks had accumulations of  2 to 3 inches of snow.  For example, the cooperative observer measured 3 inches.  The county schools were closed in Dickenson County, while Buchanan County had a 2 hour delay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system affecting the Atlantic coast was followed by a strong arctic cold front passing across the region during the early morning hours of January 3rd, resulting in brief but heavy accumulating snow confined mainly to the west-facing slopes from southeast West Virginia through the mountains of North Carolina.  Winds with the arctic surge topped 60 mph in a few places across the mountains and, when combined with snow, resulted in periods of localized blizzard-like conditions.  Temperatures dropped into the teens and the positive single digits across the mountains, resulting flash-freezing of wet surfaces from rainfall on January 2nd.  Wind chills across the mountains were exceptionally cold, dropping into the negative teens.  Gradient winds began to relax by sunrise on the 3rd, allowing snow shower activity and blowing snow to diminish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds took down trees across Montgomery County.  One tree was blown down 2 miles west of Sowers, and another tree was blown down 1 mile east of Plum Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of arctic air arrived into the mid-Atlantic during the early morning hours of January 6th. Temperatures continued to fall over the next 24 hours, eventually bottoming out below zero nearly everywhere west of the Blue Ridge.  Winds remained gusty through that 24 hour period as well due to a tightened pressure gradient as high pressure built into the region. The result was dangerously low wind chill temperatures not see in the region in many years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures were observed in the -20F to -34F range at several locations throughout the county during the early morning hours of January 7th, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet over parts of the western Virginia piedmont. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid to late afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along parts of the New River and upper Roanoke valleys.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches along and east of a Martinsville to Lynchburg line, 10 to 14 inches across the Mountain Empire part of southwest Virginia, 12 to 16 inches just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge and north into the southern Shenandoah valley, with 16 to 26 inches in an area between Covington Virginia south into the Blacksburg to Roanoke region and farther south to near Galax. The highest end of this range was centered over Floyd County.||Sleet amounts were generally less than an inch between Martinsville and Danville. Freezing rain occurred mainly along and east of a line from Martinsville to Buckingham. Amounts ranged from around one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 7 inches across the eastern part of the county to around 11 inches in the west. Freezing rain accretion averaged one to two tenths across central and eastern parts of the county with amounts a little over one-quarter of an inch in the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moving northward along the coast produced between four and ten inches of snow across portions of central and eastern Virginia from Wednesday afternoon, February 12th into Thursday evening, February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between four inches and six inches across the county. Dolphin reported 5.5 inches of snow. Brodnax reported 5.1 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous auto accidents were reported across the Piedmont of Virginia into the New River Valley as a weak wedge of high pressure allowed temperatures across much of the area to fall into the low 30s and upper 20s. An upper trough approaching from the west drew warm moisture from the Gulf of Mexico northward along the Appalachian chain to overrun the colder air at the surface. This resulted in a light freezing rain event across much of the forecast region. Because of very cold temperatures just before the event, the ground was very cold, which allowed the ice to adhere to roadway surfaces. In many instances, motorists could not tell they were traveling across an icy surface until it was too late, resulting in numerous auto accidents. Temperatures warmed above freezing for most locations by late morning, although slick roads were reported into the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kristine D. Kitts, 19, was traveling south along the 5900 block of Boonesboro Road around 7 a.m. when she lost control and ran off the left side of the road, police say. Police say Kitts was driving a 2005 Suzuki Sport Utility Vehicle. The SUV crossed a driveway and went over an embankment before turning over, police said. Investigators say Kitts was not wearing her seatbelt and was thrown from the SUV. She was pronounced dead at the scene.  Icy road conditions at the time of the crash appear to be the contributing factor.
